intro_script: This is a Testora Studio Listening Practice Test. The test will begin in five seconds.

events.speaker: Narrator
events.text: This is a Testora Studio Listening Practice Test. The test will begin in five seconds.

events.speaker: Narrator
events.text: Section One.

events.speaker: Maya
events.text: Good afternoon, Harbour Repair Workshop. Maya speaking. How can I help?

events.speaker: Naomi
events.text: Hello. I would like to book a place on the beginners' bicycle maintenance session.

events.speaker: Maya
events.text: Certainly. Can I take your surname? Patel, that's P A T E L.

events.speaker: Naomi
events.text: Yes, that's right. I saw two dates online, but I was not sure which still had spaces.

events.speaker: Maya
events.text: The page briefly showed the seventeenth of July, but that session is now reserved for a school group.

events.speaker: Maya
events.text: The public beginners' session is on the eighteenth of July, and there are three places left.

events.speaker: Naomi
events.text: Perfect. I finish work at five thirty, so I hope it is not too early.

events.speaker: Maya
events.text: You will be fine. The workshop starts at six thirty and the doors remain open until six twenty-five.

events.speaker: Naomi
events.text: Do I need to bring tools? I have a small pump, but nothing for removing a wheel.

events.speaker: Maya
events.text: We provide all the tools, and an apron is included as well.

events.speaker: Maya
events.text: You only need to bring gloves. Ordinary gardening ones are suitable.

events.speaker: Naomi
events.text: Good. What does the session cost?

events.speaker: Maya
events.text: It is forty-two pounds, including replacement cables to practise with. There is no separate deposit.

events.speaker: Naomi
events.text: I was planning to drive. Is there parking behind the workshop?

events.speaker: Maya
events.text: There are only two spaces and both are needed for staff vans. The train station is a long walk.

events.speaker: Naomi
events.text: In that case I will take the bus. Route twelve stops across the road, doesn't it?

events.speaker: Maya
events.text: It does. The main doors will be locked by then because the shop closes at six.

events.speaker: Maya
events.text: Use the side entrance, beside the blue loading bay. We will put a workshop sign there.

events.speaker: Naomi
events.text: Could you text me if anything changes?

events.speaker: Maya
events.text: Of course. I have zero seven nine two zero, six zero seven nine, from your online enquiry.

events.speaker: Naomi
events.text: The last four digits should be six zero seven four, not six zero seven nine.

events.speaker: Maya
events.text: Thanks, I have corrected that. We also invite people to bring one damaged object for the recycling demonstration.

events.speaker: Maya
events.text: A steel or aluminium part is useful, but please bring aluminium if you have a choice because it is easier to cut safely.

events.speaker: Naomi
events.text: I have an old aluminium bottle cage. That should work.

events.speaker: Maya
events.text: Yes. I will email the booking confirmation today. If you need to cancel, reply to that email at least twenty-four hours beforehand.

events.speaker: Narrator
events.text: Section Two.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: Welcome to Northhaven Coast Discovery Centre. Before you explore, I will describe the site using the map on your screen.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: You are standing at the entrance at the bottom of the map. The main path runs straight towards the shore.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: Reception is on the right as you enter, but you do not need to go there if you already have a ticket.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: The ticket kiosk is immediately on your left, at point B, before the path reaches the first junction.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: Continue to the wooden footbridge. Just after crossing it, a narrow path turns right towards the reed beds.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: Follow that narrow path to point D, where you will find the bird hide.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: Back on the main path, the education garden is beside the pond.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: The picnic shelter is opposite the garden at point F, not beside the pond as an older leaflet suggests.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: For the kayak launch, keep going until the main path bends left around the laboratory.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: Take the short path on the seaward side of that bend. The kayak point is H, close to the beach.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: The toilets are not in the laboratory. Walk past the laboratory and the outdoor showers.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: They are at point G, at the end of the paved path, just before the dunes.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: Now, a few suggestions for planning your visit.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: Families often head directly to the beach, while the short film is popular when people are tired.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: For younger children, however, begin at the touch pool. A guide is always there to help them handle the animals gently.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: The seal talk normally starts at two o'clock on weekdays.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: Today the rescue team is returning late from a survey, so the talk cannot begin at its usual time.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: It has been moved to two thirty. The feeding demonstration will follow at three.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: You may photograph wildlife and use binoculars throughout the site.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: Please do not feed the birds, even with food bought in our cafe, because it changes their natural behaviour.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: Our outdoor programme changes with the weather.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: Light rain does not stop the shore walk, and in heavy rain we use the covered observation deck.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: Strong wind is different: the shore path closes, and the activity moves into the indoor laboratory.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: Entry to the centre is free. You may see a suggested donation at reception.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: That donation pays for fuel and maintenance for the small rescue boat, rather than the exhibits inside the centre.

events.speaker: Narrator
events.text: Section Three.

events.speaker: Dr Okafor
events.text: Let's review your proposed study of food waste in the campus dining hall. What have you decided so far?

events.speaker: Mina
events.text: We first planned to compare all three dining halls, but that would give us too much data for a six-week project.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: I still think two sites would make the results stronger.

events.speaker: Mina
events.text: I would rather study one hall properly. A shallow comparison would not tell the catering team what to change.

events.speaker: Dr Okafor
events.text: A single site is acceptable if you explain the limitation.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: My concern is that students may behave differently when they notice us measuring what they leave.

events.speaker: Mina
events.text: We could put the scales behind the tray-return screen, so nobody sees the measurements.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: That solves part of it, and I agree that we should avoid questioning people while they eat.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: But I still want short interviews afterwards, because the weights alone cannot explain why food was left.

events.speaker: Mina
events.text: I offered to conduct the interviews because I wrote the draft questions.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: You also know the weighing equipment. I have used interview software before and can code the responses faster.

events.speaker: Mina
events.text: Fair point. You take the interviews, then, and I will manage the measurements instead.

events.speaker: Dr Okafor
events.text: Good. Make sure your categories separate food that was unpopular from portions that were simply too large.

events.speaker: Mina
events.text: Portion size matters because the same serving is given to everyone.

events.speaker: Dr Okafor
events.text: Exactly. That is the strongest reason to record meal type as well as total weight; soup and bread cannot be compared as if they were identical.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: Should our title be 'How much food do students waste'?

events.speaker: Dr Okafor
events.text: That only describes quantity, while your interviews investigate motives.

events.speaker: Dr Okafor
events.text: Frame the main focus as the causes of edible food waste. The total weight becomes supporting evidence.

events.speaker: Mina
events.text: For the method, we could ask diners to estimate how much they left.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: Self-reported estimates are unreliable. Photographs might be better, although classifying them would take time.

events.speaker: Dr Okafor
events.text: Use direct observation of returned plates, supported by weighing. Save interviews for explaining the patterns.

events.speaker: Mina
events.text: We tested the observation sheet with twelve diners yesterday.

events.speaker: Dr Okafor
events.text: For the main study, collect data from sixty diners across lunch and dinner. Twelve is only enough for the pilot.

events.speaker: Lucas
events.text: I can clean the spreadsheet and send it to you on Wednesday.

events.speaker: Dr Okafor
events.text: Wednesday was the date in your old plan, before the dining hall changed its menu cycle.

events.speaker: Dr Okafor
events.text: Send the final data on Thursday instead, after you have captured the vegetarian menu.

events.speaker: Mina
events.text: And our final assessment is a written report, correct?

events.speaker: Dr Okafor
events.text: The report is for the catering manager. For this module, your assessed output is a poster, which you will present in week eight.

events.speaker: Narrator
events.text: Section Four.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: Today we will look at soundscape ecology, a field that treats environmental sound as data rather than background.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: A soundscape contains biological sounds, physical sounds such as wind and water, and sounds created by people.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: The key idea is not merely to list those sounds, but to study the relationships between living things and their environment through sound.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: Animal calls perform several ecological functions.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: They can attract mates, warn of predators, coordinate a group, or defend territories.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: A recording therefore tells us more than whether a place seems pleasant to a human listener.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: When analysing a recording, loudness describes the strength of a sound.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: Frequency, by contrast, helps distinguish a low engine hum from a high insect call.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: Researchers often display both time and frequency as a coloured image.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: This image is called a spectrogram. It can reveal repeated calls that are difficult to notice by ear.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: One short recording can confirm that a species called at a particular moment, but long-term monitoring is more powerful.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: If recorders sample the same locations over months or years, researchers can compare seasonal patterns.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: Repeated detections may also provide an indicator of abundance, although they are not a direct count of individual animals.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: Human noise is one reason those comparisons require care.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: A road does not necessarily cause birds to leave the entire area.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: Traffic may instead mask their calls, making communication and detection more difficult even when the birds remain present.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: A good monitoring design reduces avoidable differences between samples.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: Recording whenever a researcher happens to be free would mix dawn, midday, and evening soundscapes.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: Recording at the same time each day makes samples more comparable and separates daily cycles from longer changes.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: Interpretation must also remain cautious.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: Suppose a frog appears in Monday's recording but not Tuesday's.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: We should say it was not recorded on Tuesday, not that it was absent. Wind or another sound may have hidden its call.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: Consider a small city park monitored beside a road.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: The weekday recordings contain steady traffic from the morning commute.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: At the same hour on weekends, traffic falls and more short bird calls can be detected. That pattern may reflect reduced masking, not an instant rise in bird numbers.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: Finally, sound recordings are valuable because they can be stored and checked again by different analysts.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: They can reveal ecological change across time, but microphones do not explain every cause.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: The strongest conclusion is therefore that acoustic evidence is a repeatable measure which must be interpreted alongside habitat, weather, and human activity.

source_note: Section 4 uses general factual background from public National Park Service educational pages; all wording, examples, questions, and audio are original Testora content.
